1: בְּנוֹת צְלָפְחָד (b'not tzlofchad, H1121+H6765): 'daughters of Zelophehad.' The five daughters are named individually — Mahlah, Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, Tirzah — an unusual honour in the legal narrative: women are granted full juridical standing as claimants. The genealogy traces through Manasseh son of Joseph, establishing their lineage in the Josephite tribes whose land allotment will fall west and east of Jordan. The naming reflects the gravity of the case: these are not anonymous suppliants but named heirs.
2: פֶּתַח אֹהֶל מוֹעֵד (petach ohel mo'ed, H6607+H168+H4150): 'entrance of the tent of meeting.' The daughters present their case at the precise threshold of covenantal adjudication — the same location where Moses meets YHWH and where Israel's legal disputes are brought for divine resolution. Standing lifnei Moses, Eleazar the priest, the leaders, and the whole congregation mirrors the full judicial structure: the case is brought before every level of authority simultaneously, ensuring its ruling will have maximum binding force.
3: חֶטְאוֹ (chet'o, H2399): 'his own sin.' The daughters carefully dissociate their father from Korah's rebellion — he died for his own sin, not in solidarity with an act of communal apostasy. This distinction matters legally: property forfeited by rebels (Korah's company) might be treated differently from estates of those who simply died in the wilderness. The daughters are not petitioning on behalf of a rebel's legacy but for an ordinary estate. benim lo-hayu lo (he had no sons) — the problem is purely structural within the inheritance code, not moral.
4: יִגָּרַע (yigara', H1639): 'be diminished/blotted out.' The daughters articulate the theological stakes: if their father's name disappears from his clan, his identity — his shem — is erased from Israel. In Hebrew thought, the survival of a name is closely linked to the survival of a person's legacy and participation in covenant community. The nachalah (H5159, inheritance/holding) is not merely economic property but the concrete, land-based form of a man's covenantal identity in Israel. To lose it is to lose presence in YHWH's land.
5: וַיַּקְרֵב מֹשֶׁה (vayakriv moshe, H7126): 'Moses brought/drew near their case.' The verb qarav (draw near, approach) is the same root as qorban (offering). Moses performs an act structurally analogous to presenting an offering: he brings the daughters' legal claim into YHWH's presence for adjudication. This framing treats legal justice as an act of drawing near to God — juridical process and priestly access share the same vocabulary.
6: וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל-מֹשֶׁה (vay'daber YHWH el-moshe): 'YHWH spoke to Moses.' The divine adjudication is direct and immediate. YHWH does not deliberate or refer the case further — the ruling comes as a clear word. This reflects the characterization of Moses's unique access to YHWH (Numbers 12:6-8: face to face, not in riddles). The legal case becomes a direct encounter between human petition and divine word.
7: כֵּן (ken, H3651): 'rightly/thus.' YHWH's single-word juridical affirmation: the daughters' claim is ken — correct, right, properly ordered. The fullest possible validation. נָתֹן תִּתֵּן (naton titen, H5414): 'you shall certainly give' — the infinitive absolute before the finite verb creates an emphatic command with no qualification. YHWH overturns the patrilineal default not by abolishing it but by extending nachalah to daughters when there are no sons. The inheritance passes ba'avotam (among their father's brothers — i.e., into the same tribe), preserving tribal integrity.
8: חֻקַּת מִשְׁפָּט (chuqat mishpat, H2708+H4941): 'a statute of judgment / a legal ordinance.' YHWH elevates the daughters' case into permanent legislative precedent. Chuqah denotes a binding statute (as opposed to a case-by-case ruling); mishpat is the legal verdict or judicial decision. The daughter-inheritance clause becomes codified law for all Israel: if a man dies with no son, his inheritance passes to his daughter. The daughters' boldness in approaching the tent of meeting produces lasting structural change in Israelite law.
9: אֶחָיו (echav, H251): 'his brothers.' The inheritance cascade continues: no son → daughter (Numbers 27:8); no daughter → brothers. The cascade logic follows the concentric circles of family solidarity: immediate descendants, then lateral kin. Each step tests whether the name and estate can be preserved within the tightest available covenantal unit before passing outward.
10: אֲחֵי אָבִיו (achei aviv, H251+H1): 'his father's brothers.' No brothers → father's brothers. The system is designed to exhaust every possible route of preservation before the name/estate falls out of the immediate family network. The phrase la'achuzzat nachalah recurs — 'as a holding of inheritance' — emphasizing that what passes is not mere property but covenantal estate.
11: שְׁאֵרוֹ הַקָּרֹב (she'ero haqarov, H7607+H7138): 'his nearest relative.' The final fallback — if father's brothers are absent, the nearest kinsman receives. She'er (flesh-relation, near-kin) is the same root that underlies the go'el (kinsman-redeemer) system. The inheritance law is not an arbitrary cascade but a structured expression of the kin-solidarity that permeates Israel's covenantal social order. The ordinance closes with the signature formula: chuqat mishpat — a statute of judgment for all Israel through all generations.
12: הָרֵי הָעֲבָרִים (harei ha'avarim, H2022+H5682): 'mountains of Abarim.' The range east of Jordan from which Moses will see Canaan — the same geography as Nebo/Pisgah (Deuteronomy 32:49; Numbers 34:1). YHWH's command is not punishment but consolation: re'eh (see it), the visual possession of what Moses cannot enter. In Hebrew covenantal thought, seeing the land is itself meaningful — a form of having arrived, even if not of entering. YHWH honours Moses's life's work by granting him sight of the goal.
13: נֶאֱסַפְתָּ אֶל-עַמֶּיךָ (ne'esafta el-amekha, H622): 'you will be gathered to your people.' The death-euphemism used for both Aaron (Numbers 20:24) and the patriarchs. Asaf (gather, receive in) frames death as homecoming rather than extinction — the dead join the company of covenant ancestors. The formula acknowledges continuity beyond individual life: Moses will be asher — received into — the corporate identity of Israel's dead.
14: מְרִיתֶם אֶת-פִּי (meritem et-pi, H4784): 'you rebelled against my command/mouth.' Marah (to be bitter, to rebel) is the root: Moses and Aaron failed at Meribah-kadesh (merivat kadesh) — the site whose name encodes the quarrel (merivah, H4808). לֹא-קִדַּשְׁתֶּם אוֹתִי (lo-qidashtem oti, H6942): 'you did not sanctify/hallow me.' The specific failure: striking the rock rather than speaking to it (Numbers 20:8-11) failed to hiqdish YHWH before Israel — failed to make him holy in the sight of the congregation. Moses's punishment is the consequence of a public act of unfaithfulness to the specific command given.
15: וַיְדַבֵּר מֹשֶׁה אֶל-יְהוָה (vay'daber moshe el-YHWH): 'Moses spoke TO YHWH.' The preposition is noteworthy: Moses speaks el (toward, to) YHWH — prayer, petition, address. His response to the announcement of his death is not protest or bargaining but intercession for the community. This is the measure of Moses's greatness: even as his own death is pronounced, his first utterance is concern for the flock he will leave behind.
16: אֱלֹהֵי הָרוּחֹת לְכָל-בָּשָׂר (elohei haruchot lekhol basar, H430+H7307+H1320): 'God of the spirits of all flesh.' This extraordinary compound divine title appears only twice in Numbers: here and at Numbers 16:22 (Moses and Aaron's intercession before Korah's judgment). The epithet asserts YHWH's sovereignty over every living being's inner animating principle — every ruach (spirit/breath) in every embodied creature belongs to him. Moses invokes this title to ground his request for a shepherd: the God who holds every ruach can certainly designate one man to carry the community's weight.
17: אֲשֶׁר-יֵצֵא לִפְנֵיהֶם וַאֲשֶׁר יָבֹא לִפְנֵיהֶם (asher yetze lifneihem va'asher yavo lifneihem): 'who will go out before them and come in before them.' The phrase is the standard idiom for military/civic leadership: leading troops into and out of battle, going ahead of the people in all their movements. Moses describes a shepherd-king. כְּצֹאן אֲשֶׁר אֵין-לָהֶם רֹעֶה (ketszon asher ein lahem ro'eh): 'like sheep that have no shepherd' — the image Jesus will use for Israel in Matthew 9:36 and Mark 6:34. Moses is praying for what becomes the central shepherd-imagery of the prophets and Gospels.
18: אִישׁ אֲשֶׁר-רוּחַ בּוֹ (ish asher ruach bo, H7307): 'a man in whom the Spirit resides/is.' YHWH's criterion for leadership is not lineage, age, or military record but the presence of ruach. Joshua had been Moses's aide since youth (Exodus 24:13; Numbers 33:11) and was one of the faithful spies (Numbers 14:6-9). But the qualification named here is internal — the ruach within him. סָמַכְתָּ אֶת-יָדְךָ עָלָיו (samakhta et-yadkha alav, H5564): 'lay your hand on him' — singular yadkha (your hand), marking this as a partial transfer of authority, contrasted with the bilateral laying of both hands at Leviticus 16:21.
19: וְהַעֲמַדְתָּ אֹתוֹ לִפְנֵי (veha'amadta oto lifnei, H5975): 'present/station him before.' Joshua is to be presented before Eleazar the priest and the whole congregation — the same audience as the daughters of Zelophehad (Numbers 27:2). Tzivah (H6680, commission/charge) — Moses is to charge/commission Joshua publicly, le'eineihem (in their eyes/sight). The public installation is essential: authority transferred privately would carry less weight; the congregation must witness the moment of transference.
20: מֵהוֹדְךָ עָלָיו (mehodkha alav, H1935): 'some of your splendor/authority upon him.' Hod (splendor, majesty, authority) — the word used of royal dignity and divine majesty (Psalms 104:1; Numbers 145:5). The mem prefix is partitive: some (not all) of Moses's authority is transferred. The text signals continuity-with-diminution: Joshua will be Israel's leader but will operate through Eleazar's Urim rather than direct face-to-face divine communication. A new era begins with a different mode of divine access.
21: וְלִפְנֵי אֶלְעָזָר הַכֹּהֵן יַעֲמֹד וְשָׁאַל לוֹ בְּמִשְׁפַּט הָאוּרִים (...umishpat ha'urim, H224): 'he shall inquire for him by the judgment of the Urim.' Moses communicated with YHWH directly (Numbers 12:8: face to face, not in riddles); Joshua will consult through the high priest's Urim. The shift is significant: the prophetic immediacy of Moses's era gives way to mediated, priestly oracular inquiry. The mishpat of the Urim — the judicial decision rendered through the ephod — becomes Joshua's mode of receiving divine guidance. The transition embodies a shift in the nature of leadership itself.
22: וַיַּעַשׂ מֹשֶׁה כַּאֲשֶׁר צִוָּה יְהוָה אֹתוֹ (vaya'as moshe ka'asher tzivah YHWH oto): 'Moses did as YHWH commanded him.' The completion formula that closes nearly every significant Mosaic action throughout the Pentateuch (cf. Exodus 40:16; Numbers 17:11). Moses's obedience here is particularly poignant: having just heard his own death sentence, he immediately carries out the very act that installs his successor. Faithfulness to YHWH's command takes precedence over personal grief.
23: וַיִּסְמֹךְ אֶת-יָדָיו עָלָיו (vayismok et-yadav alav, H5564): 'he laid his hands on him.' The act of commissioning is complete. Samakh (to support, lean, lay) — the laying of hands transfers weight, authority, and identity from the one laying to the one receiving. The narrative closes with the double citation: 'as the LORD commanded through Moses' — the closing formula matches the opening command. The authority passes lawfully, through the prophetic-priestly chain, grounded in YHWH's own appointment. Joshua's succession is not a human promotion but a divine installation.
